common_name: Java Olive (Tropical Chestnut, Bastard Poon Tree)

planting.note: Plant container stock or seeds in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; Java olive is a large tropical tree of the Old World tropics producing distinctive, star-shaped, woody, red seed pods that split open to reveal black, edible, oily seeds — one of the most dramatic ornamental seed displays of any tropical tree.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; a fast-growing tree that requires ample space. Sterculia foetida (Java olive or poon tree) is a large, fast-growing tropical tree native from East Africa and India through Southeast Asia to northern Australia, producing an impressive display of distinctive, star-shaped, woody, bright-red seed pods 4–6 inches across that split open dramatically to reveal 5–18 shiny black seeds with a high (55%) oil content. The common name 'Java olive' refers to the traditional use of the seeds as cooking oil and food in Java and other Indonesian islands — the seeds have a mild, slightly bitter, nutty flavor when roasted and can be pressed for a high-quality edible oil similar to olive oil in fatty acid composition. CAUTION: Raw seeds contain cyclopropene fatty acids that are mildly toxic; seeds should be roasted or heated before consumption. The tree has striking large, palmate leaves and produces flowers with a distinctive unpleasant odor ('foetida' = stinking) that attracts pollinating flies. An unusual specimen tree for large tropical landscapes where few food trees provide such dramatic seed pod displays.
sources: Purdue University — New Crop Resources — Sterculia foetida
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Trees

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile, deep soil in part shade to full sun; bacupari is a slow-growing, understory-adapted tropical fruit tree related to mangosteen that thrives in the shade of larger trees in tropical gardens and fruiting landscapes.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in part shade to full sun; bacupari is slow-growing and may take 8–10 years to fruit from seed. Garcinia madruno (bacupari or madruno) is a Colombian and South American tropical fruit tree in the mangosteen family (Clusiaceae/Guttiferae) — related to the famous mangosteen (G. mangostana) and native to the cloud forests and tropical lowlands of Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Venezuela. It produces small (1–2 inch), oval, bright-yellow to orange fruits when ripe, with thin, slightly resinous skin and white, juicy pulp with 1–3 seeds — the flavor is sweet-tart with floral-citrus notes similar to mangosteen but somewhat less complex. The tree is adapted to partial shade as an understory species in its native cloud forest habitat, making it one of the few tropical fruit trees that can be grown productively in partial shade. Bacupari/madruno is virtually unknown outside of Colombia and the Andean region where it is grown as a local specialty fruit. For zone 11–12 tropical gardeners seeking the most unusual and rare edible collectors' plants. Extremely rare in commercial availability.
sources: Purdue University — New Crop Resources — Tropical Garcinia
Universidad Nacional de Colombia — Frutas Amazónicas

common_name: Japanese Pepper Vine (Fuji Kadsura, Asian Pepper Vine)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in part shade to full shade; Japanese pepper vine is a tropical Asian climbing vine related to black pepper that grows as an understory climber in humid, shaded forest conditions — it can be used as a shade-tolerant ornamental and culinary herb in warm gardens.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in part to full shade; provide trellis or tree trunk support. Piper kadsura (Japanese pepper vine) is a Japanese and East Asian climbing vine in the pepper family (Piperaceae) — related to true black pepper (P. nigrum) but native to the humid, shaded forests of Japan, China, and Korea. The plant produces attractive, heart-shaped, deeply veined, dark-green leaves with conspicuous venation and a mildly spicy pepper aroma when crushed. Small, string-like flower spikes produce tiny, red to dark-purple peppercorns-like berries in fall that are mildly peppery-spicy and have been used as a seasoning and traditional medicine ingredient in East Asian cuisine and herbal practice. The vine climbs by adhesive stem roots similar to Virginia creeper and can attach to rough stone, brick, and wood surfaces. As a shade-tolerant ornamental vine for zone 9–12, it provides year-round interest with its attractive foliage and subtle fruiting display. Used in Japanese herbal medicine (kampo) for joint pain and circulation. Extremely rare in Western horticulture.
sources: Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online
Japanese Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries
University of Hawaii Cooperative Extension — Tropical Pipers
